codeSnapshot.label: collaborative_filtering.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse import csr_matrix
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

# ── 1. User-based Collaborative Filtering ──
# R: user-item rating matrix (n_users × n_items), 0 = unrated
R = np.array([
    [5, 3, 0, 1],
    [4, 0, 4, 1],
    [1, 1, 0, 5],
    [0, 0, 5, 4],
    [0, 0, 4, 4],
])

# Mean-centre each user's ratings to remove rating scale bias
R_mean = np.true_divide(R.sum(1), (R != 0).sum(1))  # per-user mean
R_centred = R.copy().astype(float)
for u in range(R.shape[0]):
    mask = R[u] != 0
    R_centred[u, mask] -= R_mean[u]

# Cosine similarity between users (on rated items only via centred matrix)
user_sim = cosine_similarity(R_centred)  # (n_users, n_users)

# Predict rating for user u, item i: weighted average of similar users
def predict_user_cf(u, i, R, user_sim, k=3):
    sim_u = user_sim[u].copy()
    sim_u[u] = 0           # exclude self
    # Only use users who rated item i
    rated_mask = R[:, i] != 0
    sim_u[~rated_mask] = 0
    top_k = np.argsort(sim_u)[-k:]
    num = sum(user_sim[u, v] * (R[v, i] - R_mean[v]) for v in top_k)
    den = sum(abs(user_sim[u, v]) for v in top_k) + 1e-9
    return R_mean[u] + num / den

print(predict_user_cf(u=0, i=2, R=R, user_sim=user_sim))  # predict user 0, item 2

# ── 2. Item-based CF (more scalable — item similarity matrix is stable) ──
item_sim = cosine_similarity(R_centred.T)  # (n_items, n_items)

def predict_item_cf(u, i, R, item_sim, k=3):
    rated_by_u = np.where(R[u] != 0)[0]
    sims = item_sim[i, rated_by_u]
    top_k_idx = np.argsort(sims)[-k:]
    top_k_items = rated_by_u[top_k_idx]
    num = sum(item_sim[i, j] * R[u, j] for j in top_k_items)
    den = sum(abs(item_sim[i, j]) for j in top_k_items) + 1e-9
    return num / den

# ── 3. ALS Matrix Factorization (implicit feedback) — via implicit library ──
# pip install implicit
import implicit
from scipy.sparse import csr_matrix

# Confidence matrix C = 1 + α * R (where R = interaction counts)
alpha = 40
confidence = csr_matrix(1 + alpha * R)

model = implicit.als.AlternatingLeastSquares(factors=50, regularization=0.1, iterations=20)
model.fit(confidence.T)  # implicit expects (item, user)

# Recommend items for user 0
ids, scores = model.recommend(userid=0, user_items=confidence[0], N=5, filter_already_liked_items=True)
print('Recommended items:', ids)

eli3: Collaborative Filtering (CF) recommends items based on what similar users liked — 'people like you also enjoyed X.' It finds patterns in a user-item matrix of ratings or interactions: if user A and user B rated the same movies similarly, and A loved a movie B hasn't seen, recommend it to B. No item content is needed — only the collective behaviour of the user population.

There are two flavours: user-based CF (find similar users, borrow their preferences) and item-based CF (find items similar to what you've liked before, recommend similar ones). Item-based CF is more scalable for large platforms — item similarity changes slowly compared to user similarity, so it can be pre-computed.

Memory-based CF (computing raw similarity) struggles with sparse matrices — most users rate only a handful of items out of thousands. Model-based CF (matrix factorization: SVD, ALS, BPR) solves this by learning dense latent factor vectors for users and items that generalize across unobserved interactions.

whenWhy.title: Cold-start-free recommendations for established users
whenWhy.body: CF works best when users have interaction history — purchases, views, ratings, clicks. Once a user has at least 5–10 interactions, CF can identify similar users or items and generate useful recommendations. For established platforms (Netflix, Spotify, Amazon) with millions of users and dense interaction histories, CF is the backbone of the recommendation pipeline.

whenWhy.title: No item metadata required
whenWhy.body: CF is purely behavioural — it doesn't need item descriptions, genres, or features. This makes it applicable wherever metadata is absent, noisy, or proprietary. A playlist recommendation system can leverage listening patterns without needing song tags. A B2B product recommendation system can leverage purchase histories without product descriptions.

whenWhy.title: Discovering cross-domain preferences
whenWhy.body: CF surfaces non-obvious connections — 'people who bought this technical book also bought this notebook brand.' These emergent cross-category associations are impossible with content-based filtering (which looks only at item similarity) and are the primary source of recommendation surprise and serendipity that drives user engagement.

deepDiveIntro: Memory-based user CF predicts a user's rating for an item by taking a weighted average of ratings from similar users, with similar users contributing more. Pearson correlation is standard for explicit ratings because it accounts for individual rating scale differences (a stingy rater and a generous rater can still agree on relative rankings). Item-based CF uses the same idea but swaps the roles: predict a user's rating for an item based on how they rated similar items. Item similarity is preferred in production because item similarity matrices are far smaller and more stable than user similarity matrices at scale. [1]

Model-based CF via matrix factorisation compresses the user-item rating matrix into two dense low-dimensional matrices representing hidden user preferences and item characteristics. For implicit feedback data (clicks, views, purchases) where a zero means 'not yet seen' rather than 'disliked', the ALS algorithm assigns a confidence weight to each interaction: more interactions mean higher confidence. ALS alternates between solving for user factors with items fixed and item factors with users fixed, each update having a closed-form solution. [2]

deepDive.heading: Pearson Correlation vs Cosine Similarity
deepDive.body: Pearson correlation captures whether two users agree on relative rankings, even if their absolute rating scales differ — a user who gives 4s and 5s can still correlate highly with one who gives 1s and 2s if they rank items the same way. Raw cosine similarity doesn't account for this scale difference, but subtracting each user's mean rating before computing cosine (called adjusted cosine) makes it equivalent to Pearson. For binary implicit feedback (viewed or not viewed), the Jaccard index — the fraction of items both users interacted with out of all items either did — is the standard similarity measure. [1]

deepDive.heading: ALS for Implicit Feedback
deepDive.body: Implicit feedback (clicks, views, purchase counts) is trickier than star ratings because a zero does not mean 'disliked' — it could simply mean the user has not been exposed to that item yet. The ALS approach treats all zero entries as weak negative signals and assigns a confidence weight proportional to interaction count: one click is low confidence, many purchases is high confidence. Crucially, the loss sums over every user-item pair (not just observed interactions) because unobserved pairs are treated as low-confidence negatives. Each update of user or item factors given the other side fixed has a closed-form solution, making ALS fast and parallelisable. [2]
deepDive.formula: x_u = (YᵀC_uY + λI)⁻¹Yᵀc_up_u   [ALS user update, closed form]

deepDive.heading: Scalability and the Netflix Prize Legacy
deepDive.body: Computing user-user similarity at Netflix scale (hundreds of millions of users) is completely infeasible — the similarity matrix would never fit in memory. Item-based CF scales much better because the item catalogue is much smaller and similarity can be pre-computed offline; at serving time you just look up the most similar items to what the user has already liked. Matrix factorisation-based approaches (ALS) scale even better: inference reduces to a single dot product per item. The Netflix Prize showed that blending many CF variants with content features produces the best recommendations, which is why modern systems are almost always hybrid. [1]

takeaways: User-based CF: predict r̂_{u,i} from weighted average of similar users' ratings. Pearson/adjusted cosine similarity. [1]
Item-based CF: more scalable (stable item-item similarity, pre-computable). Preferred over user-based in production. [1]
ALS (implicit feedback): confidence c_{u,i} = 1+α·r_{u,i}; closed-form user/item updates. All (u,i) pairs in the loss. [2]
Cold-start problem: CF fails for new users/items with no interactions. Hybrid with content-based features mitigates. [1]
For large-scale: ANN search (FAISS/HNSW) for user-user or item-item similarity; ALS for factorization. [2]

edgeCases.q: How does CF handle the cold-start problem for new users or items?
edgeCases.a: Cold-start is CF's main weakness: a new user with no interactions has no similarity to other users; a new item with no ratings appears in no one's neighbourhood. Mitigations: (1) New user: prompt for initial ratings on popular 'anchor' items during onboarding — even 5 ratings enable CF. (2) New item: use content-based filtering until sufficient interactions accumulate, then blend with CF (hybrid approach). (3) Bayesian average: initialize new item ratings at the global mean rather than 0, so the item appears in some recommendations immediately. (4) Warm-start: for matrix factorization, initialize new user/item vectors using the average of existing factors for items they've interacted with or content features.

edgeCases.q: Why does user-based CF degrade on large platforms while item-based CF remains practical?
edgeCases.a: At Netflix scale (200M+ users), the user-user similarity matrix is 200M × 200M — computationally infeasible to compute or store. The item-item similarity matrix is n_items × n_items (e.g., 10,000 × 10,000 for a movie catalogue), which is small and stable. Item similarity doesn't change as fast as user preferences — it can be recomputed daily or weekly. At recommendation time, given a user's watch history (a few hundred items), find the K most similar items to each watched item and aggregate scores — all matrix lookups, no new similarity computation. This is why Amazon's 2003 item-based CF paper 'Amazon.com Recommendations' became a production standard.

sources.label: Koren, Y., Bell, R. & Volinsky, C. — Matrix Factorization Techniques for Recommender Systems, IEEE Computer 42(8), 2009
Hu, Y., Koren, Y. & Volinsky, C. — Collaborative Filtering for Implicit Feedback Datasets, IEEE ICDM 2008
